title: SMSF lending ban contradicts the budget it was bolted onto
full_text:
In policymaking, the first test should always be simple: does the measure solve the problem it is designed to address?
That is the question the government must now answer, following its move to ban new lending for residential property within self-managed super funds (SMSFs) while grandfathering existing arrangements.
The measure was not in the budget. It was not consulted on. And it will take effect just 45 days after the legislation is passed.
The stated objective is housing affordability. That is a serious national priority. The nation needs more homes and a policy framework that helps all Australians, particularly younger ones, into secure housing.
But this ill-considered ban undercuts that very goal, working against efforts to increase housing supply and affordability. At the same time, it has created real uncertainty for working Australians planning for retirement, lenders supporting competition, innovation and access to the home lending market, and the broader credit markets that fund investment across the economy.
Let’s start with the evidence because the numbers tell a story at odds with the rhetoric.
The latest data shows that SMSFs hold about $75 billion in assets supported by limited recourse borrowing arrangements (LRBAs), backed by $28.9 billion in debt. That’s in a housing market valued at just under $13 trillion.
By the government’s own reckoning, LRBAs account for less than 1 per cent of total residential property lending and less than half a per cent of new lending each year.
If the policy target is housing demand, the scale of intervention should be matched to the scale of the problem.
This is a small, specialised and well-understood market. It already operates inside a strong regulatory framework, with super trustee duties, superannuation balance constraints and structural safeguards that limit speculative investment, and which have been examined and reviewed repeatedly.
A blanket ban is a blunt instrument for a problem of this size.
Australia’s housing problem is fundamentally a supply issue. It will not be solved by narrowing retirement savings choices for people who have chosen to take responsibility for their own superannuation savings and retirement planning. It will be solved by building more homes, encouraging investment, improving planning systems and creating stable policy settings that support confidence.
Policy contradiction is difficult to ignore
This is where the policy contradiction becomes difficult to ignore.
At the same time that the government is seeking more housing supply and stronger retirement outcomes, this measure removes a pathway some Australians use to diversify their retirement savings into residential property.
Diversification is the first principle of prudent retirement planning, and large super funds will continue to invest across asset classes, including property. This measure makes property harder to access for one category of fund alone.
The practical effect is that residential property inside superannuation becomes the preserve of those wealthy enough to buy outright – the opposite of what the policy claims to want, which is more affordable housing for more Australians.
Those most affected are not professional investors or wealthy speculators. They are working Australians in their 30s, 40s and 50s using modest super balances as a disciplined, long-term path to retirement security. They are younger Australians getting a foot into the housing market. Closing the door on them, with no notice and little transition, is no small thing.
None of this means every SMSF borrowing arrangement is right for every person. Property spruiking, poor advice and high-pressure sales tactics should be addressed directly. Consumers should never be pushed into inappropriate super switching, unsuitable borrowing, or poorly explained investment strategies.
But that is not the same as saying the product should be banned for everyone. The better policy response is targeted regulation, not a blanket restriction that removes choice from people using the framework responsibly.
“The government should take a more proportionate, targeted and sensible pathway.”
This is particularly important because the market today is not the market of a decade ago. Since the time earlier inquiries considered LRBAs, Australia has strengthened superannuation governance, SMSF oversight, macroprudential settings and lending standards. This policy revisits old arguments without sufficient consideration of current evidence.
For SMSFs, residential property is not about short-term speculation. It is part of long-term retirement planning. It is used to diversify across shares, cash, fixed income and property, helping manage risk and safeguard superannuation savings across market cycles.
Reasonable people can disagree on whether that is the right strategy. But the choice should not be removed without careful analysis of the consequences.
Those consequences extend beyond SMSF trustees. When the major banks left this part of the market years ago, non-bank lenders stepped in. They drove competition, innovation and choice in this segment of the market, and served these borrowers responsibly.
If policy settings reduce the availability of capital to these non-bank lenders, that can affect their capacity to provide finance to households and businesses more broadly. It risks chipping away at the very competition that keeps credit affordable and available for everyone else.
This matters beyond any single product. The finance industry is the transmission mechanism through which tax and housing policies reach the real economy.
Changes that affect the efficiency of capital allocation, the cost and availability of credit across lending markets and the choice of financial products and services available to ordinary Australians should be supported by proper, evidence-based policy analysis – not improvised to get a legislative package across the line.
Having proceeded with the changes, the government should consult carefully on the design and transitional arrangements. At minimum, borrowers and industry need clarity on contracts already signed, approvals already issued, settlements already scheduled, refinancing of existing LRBAs, and the operational guidance that lenders, brokers, trustees and advisers will be expected to follow.
The government should take a more proportionate, targeted and sensible pathway. Allowing borrowing for one residential property within an SMSF would preserve housing ownership and superannuation diversification. This approach would maintain clear guardrails and better align with the government’s stated policy objectives for affordable housing, first-home buyer incentives, retirement savings and intergenerational fairness.
The Australian Finance Industry Association and its members support policy settings that improve housing outcomes, protect consumers and maintain confidence in the financial system.
We are asking the government to do what good government does: consult on the design, model the consequences, and get the transition right to support working Australians now, and into the future.
Australia needs more homes and stronger retirement outcomes. The challenge is to design policies that support both.
YXJ0aWNsZTowODU1ZmQ3OS1hN2JiLTQ0NGYtYmY3Yi0zOTJiZDU2NWNkN2Q
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Diane Tate
heading: Superannuation and Investments
